deductions.reason: In several places the tutor supplied the key interpretive bridge from the data category to its climate relevance (for example, explicitly stating what climate zone, population/area, income per capita, and GDP imply about hazards or capacity) rather than first having the student derive those links.

After the student improved following earlier confusion, the tutor affirmed the answer and moved on without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's reasoning or why the new approach worked.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently stayed in a coaching role, kept the discussion anchored to the assignment, and used concise prompts plus useful source-selection/process guidance. The main weakness was that the tutor sometimes gave the core interpretation of what categories mean for climate vulnerability/capacity before the student had worked that out. I also saw missed opportunities to ask the student to reflect after a successful recovery. Some repetition was present, but there was enough reframing and forward movement that I did not deduct for spiraling.
grade.judge_reasoning: I deducted under 1.1 because the tutor occasionally performed the central reasoning step by telling the student what particular data categories imply about hazards, exposure, or capacity instead of eliciting those inferences first. I deducted under 1.3.C because, after students produced stronger answers following confusion, the tutor generally confirmed correctness and advanced without asking what changed in their thinking. I did not deduct for near-submission-ready writing, because the tutor did not draft a finished table or polished prose for submission. I also did not deduct for assignment anchoring or communication quality, since the tutor remained focused on the stated task, supportive, and mostly bite-sized throughout.
